text: The surgeon positions the suprapubic port for the transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair, following the baseball diamond concept to create a diamond shape. The ipsilateral port position is used, with the right hand port placed 7.5 centimeters lateral in the same arc, and the left hand port positioned accordingly.

The surgeon measures an 18-centimeter distance from the target site using a diamond-shaped configuration. A stab wound is made, and a Veress needle is introduced perpendicular to the lifted abdominal wall. The procedure includes hearing two click sounds, followed by irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests.

The surgeon attaches the tubing of the insufflator to begin pneumoperitoneum, with the pressure set at fifteen mmHg. The actual pressure gradually increases as gas is introduced, reaching a total of approximately 3.5 liters. The incision is then enlarged to 11 mm.

The surgeon introduces a 10 mm trocar using a screwing motion, ensuring the tip of the index finger is pointed towards the sharp end to prevent inadvertent entry or injury. A hissing sound is heard as the tubing is attached to the insufflator. The telescope is then introduced, with the patient's head positioned down and right side elevated, establishing the first working port.

The surgeon introduces the second working port for the transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair. The initial diagnostic laparoscopy begins, revealing the medial, lateral, and median umbilical ligaments. The hernia sac is visible during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully extracts the omentum from the right-sided indirect inguinal hernia sac. The telescope is inserted into the hernial sac to begin dissection, located six centimeters lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel and the outer margin of the deep ring.

The surgeon introduces a 30-degree telescope into the hernia sac, revealing the inguinal scrotal hernia. Dissection begins at the two o'clock position, six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring. One hand pulls the peritoneum while the other uses either monopolar scissors or a harmonic device to cut the peritoneum, extending medially up to the medial umbilical ligament above the arc of the defect.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection to create the preperitoneal pocket using monopolar scissors, a spatula, or a placelet. The dissection occurs over the triangle of doom, prioritizing lateral dissection before proceeding to medial dissection and finally addressing the hernia sac.

The surgeon uses the back of the scissors as a blunt dissector to create a preperitoneal pocket. The lateral dissection progresses until the spermatic vessel becomes visible, indicating completion of the lateral dissection. The left hand supports the peritoneum while the right hand separates it from the transversalis fascia, forming a pocket lateral to the Triangle of Doom.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection and begins the medial dissection, revealing the inferior epigastric vessel. As the dissection progresses, the Cooper's ligament becomes clearly visible, serving as a landmark. The space of Retzius is enlarged, with the bladder visible below. The inferior epigastric vessel is observed at the twelve o'clock position, and the space of Retzius is firmly established.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia, fully isolating the hernia sac. One hand pulls the sac while the other pushes the transversalis fascia away. Below, the spermatic vessel and vas deferens are clearly visible. The transversalis fascia is pushed back, and the peritoneum is pulled out, ensuring not to engage with the pseudosac.

The surgeon carefully separates the tissue, ensuring not to injure the vas deferens or spermatic vessels, while avoiding the creation of any buttonholes in the peritoneum. Both hands are used skillfully to manipulate the tissues.

The surgeon uses the left hand to push the transversalis fascia back into the defect while the right hand holds the peritoneum. A window is visible between the spermatic vessel and the sac, with the spermatic vessel separated from the sac. The sac is isolated and will be separated from the transversalis fascia. The spermatic vessel, also known as the testicular vessel, is identified as the lateral boundary of the triangle of doom.
The surgeon identifies the pseudo-sac, which is the transversalis fascia, and the true sac, which is the peritoneum, distinguishing between the two structures during the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon completes the separation of the peritoneum from the transversal fascia, which is pushed back into the defect by CO₂ pressure. The surgical field reveals the triangle of doom with the vas deferens and spermatic vessel clearly visible. The iliac vessel is situated between the external iliac artery and vein. The pocket is enlarged to accommodate the mesh without folding, ensuring proper peritonization and mesh placement.

The surgeon prepares to place the mesh, with visible anatomical landmarks including the bladder, Bogros space, trapezoid of disaster, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain. The mesh is folded and inserted, then spread over the defect, ensuring the center of the mesh aligns with the center of the defect. The mesh size is calculated as twelve plus the defect size, with a 15x15 cm mesh used for a 3 cm defect.

The surgeon fixes the inferomedial end of the mesh to the Cooper's ligament using a tacker, ensuring coverage of the defect, epigastric vessel, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. The mesh is pushed up and covered with the peritoneum, employing a double brass technique to ensure no part of the mesh remains visible.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for any buttonholes in the mesh and addresses them by pushing up the lower edge. The procedure concludes with a thorough check for bleeding before exiting the surgical site.
The surgeon gently compresses the scrotum to expel any residual gas, ensuring no seroma formation. Postoperative scrotal support is applied to prevent serum collection.
